coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent hollow mesoporous carbon spheres for drug loading and tumor treatment through 980-nm laser and microwave co-irradiation

Chen, Yang

coredata.dc:description: Hollow mesoporous particles for drug delivery and cancer therapy have attracted significant attention over recent decades. Here, we develop a simple and highly efficient strategy for preparing fluorescent hollow mesoporous carbon spheres (HMCSs). Compared with typical carbon materials such as fullerene C60, carbon nanotubes, reduced graphene oxide, and carbon nanohorns; HMCSs showed fewer effects on cell cycle distribution and lower toxicity to cells. Ten different drugs were incorporated into the HMCSs, and the maximum loading efficiency reached 42.79 ± 2.7%. Importantly, microwaves were found to improve the photothermal effect generated by HMCSs when combined with 980-nm laser irradiation. The cell killing and tumor growth inhibition efficiencies of HMCSs and drug-loaded HMCSs under co-irradiation with laser and microwaves were significantly improved compared with those under laser irradiation alone. After local administration HMCSs were only distributed in tissue at the injection site. HMCSs showed almost no toxicity in mice after local injection and could be completely removed from the injection site.

Stability and retention of HMCSs in mouse after local injection
Comparison of the cytotoxicity of HMCSs and four typical carbon-based materials; C60, MWCNTs, rGO, and CNHs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology of BSA spheres and HMCSs. a) Dry powders of BSA spheres and HMCSs obtained from BSA spheres under different conditions. SEM images of b) BSA spheres and HMCSs carbonized at c) 270 °C for 20 min, d) 300 °C for 20 min, and e) 300 °C for 30 min. f) TEM and g–i) HRTEM images of ultrathin sections of the HMCSs carbonized at 300 °C for 20 min [note: g–i) are images of HMCS shells]. Red arrows indicate the mesopores and green arrows indicate the carbon dots might locate in the shell. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Properties of the BSA spheres and HMCSs. a) UV–vis absorption and b) FT-IR spectra of BSA spheres and HMCSs obtained from BSA spheres carbonized under different conditions. c–f) HMCSs obtained from BSA spheres carbonized at 300 °C for 20 min: c) Energy dispersive x-ray spectrum (insert: elemental composition of HMCSs), d) Raman spectrum, e) XRD pattern, and f) mesoporous size distribution. g) True-color fluorescence images and h) fluorescence spectra of water-dispersed HMCSs carbonized under different conditions. i,j) Confocal images of HMCSs carbonized at 300 °C for 20 min: i) low magnification, j) high magnification [a consecutive series of overlays of fluorescence and bright-field images scanned from the top of the HMCSs to the bottom (excitation filter: 365 nm, emission filter: 400–600 nm)]. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Properties of the fluorescent carbon dots extracted from the HMCSs by sonication. a) Aqueous solutions and b) fluorescence spectra of carbon dots extracted from the HMCSs by sonication for 5, 30, and 60 min (excitation: 365 nm). c) Fluorescence spectra of carbon dots extracted from the HMCSs by sonication for 60 min at different excitation wavelengths. d, e) HRTEM images of the carbon dots: d: low magnification, e: high magnification. The red circle indicates a single carbon dot. f) Intracellular ROS levels generated by the carbon dots under 671-nm and 980-nm laser irradiation. The significance level observed was ***P < 0.001, compared with the PBS group. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Comparison of the cytotoxicity of HMCSs and C60, MWCNTs, rGO, and CNHs; and drug loading capacity of HMCSs. a) Viabilities of CNE cancer cells incubated with 0.1 and 0.4 mg/mL HMCSs for 24 h. b) Representative images of the CNE cancer cell cycle analyzed by flow cytometry. c) CNE cell cycle distribution histograms obtained by flow cytometry. For b) and c), the CNE cells were incubated with HMCSs at 0.4 mg/mL for 24 h. d) Loading efficiency of ten different drugs in HMCSs. In this study, the drug loading efficiency is equal to the encapsulation efficiency. Confocal images of e, f) SCC-HMCSs (insert: high magnification image of a single particle) and d) HMCSs, g) is a consecutive series of overlays of fluorescence and bright-field images scanned from the top of the SCC-HMCSs to the bottom (excitation filter: 488 nm, emission filter: 550–650 nm). ***P or # P < 0.001, **P < 0.01, and *P < 0.05 indicate statistical differences compared with the untreated group.

980-nm laser-induced photothermal conversion of HMCSs in aqueous solution or in mouse tumor with and without the assistance of microwave irradiation, and controls. HMCSs were stabilized by F127 (15 wt%). For measurement of photothermal conversion in aqueous solution, the concentration and volume of water-dispersed HMCSs were 1 or 0.5 mg/mL and 200 μL, respectively. For measurement of photothermal conversion in tumor, each tumor was injected with 50 μL of HMCSs (5 mg/mL). HMCSs in a) aqueous solution or b) mouse tumor were irradiated with the 980-nm laser for 20 min. HMCSs in c) aqueous solution or d) mouse tumor were irradiated with microwaves for 20 min. HMCSs in e) aqueous solution or f) mouse tumor were irradiated with microwaves until the suspension or tumor increased to and was maintained at a certain temperature, and were then co-irradiated with a 980-nm laser and microwaves for 20 min. In Fig. a)–f), the figures on the left are infrared thermal images, and those on the right are temperature increase curves vs. irradiation time based on the images shown in the left hand figures. Inserts: schematic illustrations of the experimental methods.

Cell killing efficiency of HMCSs, DDP, and DDP-HMCSs upon 980-nm laser irradiation with and without the assistance of microwave irradiation, and controls. a) Cells were incubated with HMCSs for ~5 min, and then subjected to irradiation. b) Cells were incubated with HMCSs, DDP, or DDP-HMCSs for 24 h, and then subjected to irradiation. (Left) Schematic illustrations of the experimental methods. (Right) Cell viability assay. The concentration of HMCSs was 1 mg/mL. HMCSs were stabilized by 15 wt% F127. ***P < 0.001 indicates statistical differences compared with the PBS group or two groups shown in the figure.

The stability of HMCSs following immersion in 10% FBS for 7, 15, and 30 days. (a) SEM images. (b) Photothermal conversion stability of HMCSs: left, infrared thermal images; right, temperature increase vs. 980-nm laser irradiation time curves based on the images on the left. HMCSs without FBS treatment were set as the control (0 days).

In vivo tumor inhibition of HMCSs and DDP-HMCSs under 980-nm laser irradiation with and without the assistance of 3 W microwave irradiation, plus controls. a) Representative photographs of tumor-bearing mice in eleven different groups: “HMCSs, laser”, “HMCSs, MW”, “HMCSs, laser + MW”, “DDP-HMCSs, laser”, “DDP-HMCSs, MW”, “DDP-HMCSs”, laser + MW”, “DDP, MW”, as well as DDP-HMCSs, HMCSs, DDP, and F127 without laser or microwave irradiation. b–d) Growth curves for tumors throughout the 18 days of treatment. For convenient comparison the growth curves in c) “HMCSs, laser + MW” and “DDP-HMCSs, laser” groups, and d) “DDP-HMCSs, laser”, and “DDP-HMCSs, laser + MW” groups are listed separately. e–g) Mouse tumors at 19 days post initial irradiation (or injection) (for the groups where the tumors could no longer be detected the skin at the original tumor site was resected and is shown in this figure). e) Tumor morphology and size, f) tumor weight, and g) H&E stained images of ultra-thin sections of skin resected from the original tumor site (the images of ultra-thin sections of tumors are given in the Supporting Information (Fig. S7). Significance levels observed were ***P < 0.001, **P < 0.01, *P < 0.05, compared with the F127 group [for b) and f)], or compared with each other [for c) and d)]. n = 4 per group. MW is microwave.

The effect of exposure to HMCSs on mouse body weight and the histological structure of major organs. ICR mice were injected with HMCSs and then euthanized for analysis at 1, 7, and 30 days after injection. (a) Mouse body weight. (b) Main organ coefficient (i.e., the ratio of organ weight to body weight). (c) Representative H&E stained images of liver, spleen, lung, and kidney of HMCS-exposed mice. n = 4 per group.

The effect of exposure to HMCSs on mouse liver and kidney functions as well as routine blood indexes. ICR mice were injected with HMCSs and then euthanized for analysis at 1, 7, and 30 days after injection. (a) Serum biochemistry and (b) hematology data. n = 4 per group.

Tracking the retention of HMCSs at the injection sites in mice. (a) The retained traces and (b) 980-nm laser-triggered photothermal conversion of HMCSs at the injection sites varying with time: left, infrared thermal images; right, temperature increase vs. irradiation time curves based on the thermal images. Red arrows indicate the injection sites. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The stability of HMCSs located at the injection sites and the feasibility of cleaning HMCSs from tissue 30 days post-injection. (a) Bright field images of mouse tissues resected from the injection sites of the four mice shown in Fig. 11a. (b–c) Images of H&E stained ultra-thin sections of tissue from the injection site: (b) low, (c) middle, and (d) high magnification. (e, f) Confocal and (g) SEM images of the ultra-thin sections of HMCSs separated from the tissue at the injection site: (e) low and (f) high magnification, the insert in (g) is the high magnification image of a HMCS with residual tissue on the surface.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of HMCSs and drug-loaded HMCSs for cancer therapy induced by 980-nm laser irradiation assisted by microwave irradiation.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent hollow mesoporous carbon spheres for drug loading and tumor treatment through 980-nm laser and microwave co-irradiation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hollow mesoporous particles for drug delivery and cancer therapy have attracted significant attention over recent decades. Here, we develop a simple and highly efficient strategy for preparing fluorescent hollow mesoporous carbon spheres (HMCSs). Compared with typical carbon materials such as fullerene C60, carbon nanotubes, reduced graphene oxide, and carbon nanohorns; HMCSs showed fewer effects on cell cycle distribution and lower toxicity to cells. Ten different drugs were incorporated into the HMCSs, and the maximum loading efficiency reached 42.79 ± 2.7%. Importantly, microwaves were found to improve the photothermal effect generated by HMCSs when combined with 980-nm laser irradiation. The cell killing and tumor growth inhibition efficiencies of HMCSs and drug-loaded HMCSs under co-irradiation with laser and microwaves were significantly improved compared with those under laser irradiation alone. After local administration HMCSs were only distributed in tissue at the injection site. HMCSs showed almost no toxicity in mice after local injection and could be completely removed from the injection site.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based materials such as fullerene C60 (a quasi-zero-dimensional material) [1,2], carbon nanotubes (CNTs, one dimensional materials) [3,4], graphene oxide (GO) and reduced GO (rGO) (two dimensional materials) [5–8], and spherical carbon nanohorn aggregations (CNHs, three dimensional materials) [9–11], have attracted significant research attention in the field of cancer therapy. A main reason for this is that these carbon materials can be used as both drug delivery systems and cancer photothermal therapy (PTT) agents, which leads to their potential use as dual or multimodal cancer treatments. However, these carbon-based materials may show toxicity to cells and animals [12–15]. For example, administration of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) by inhalation can cause acute and chronic inflammation and granuloma formation in the lungs of rodents, and well-dispersed SWCNTs exhibit greater toxicity than less well dispersed analogues [12]. In addition, the preparation of these graphene-based materials can be complex and/or high cost, or require strong acid/alkali or strong oxidants [16–21]. Therefore, low toxicity or essentially non-toxic carbon materials for drug loading and cancer therapy, made using simple, safe, and ‘green’ methods, urgently need to be developed for clinical applications. A strategy for accelerating the shrinkage of tumors in the body using laser irradiation of carbon materials combined with another stimulus such as microwave irradiation is also required.

In this study, hollow mesoporous carbon spheres (HMCSs) containing fluorescent carbon dots were fabricated on a large scale using a simple and highly efficient strategy. Aqueous bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution was spray dried in air and then carbonized at high temperature under the protection of a nitrogen atmosphere (Scheme 1 ). No toxic organic solvents, strong acid or alkali, or strong oxidants were used in the procedure. The HMCSs obtained exhibited high purity and showed lower cytotoxicity than C60, MWCNTs, rGO, and CNHs. BSA was selected as a precursor because it is low cost compared with many other proteins, so the fluorescent carbon materials can be synthesized on a large scale. In addition, BSA can be easily dissolved in water, and BSA molecules are stable in aqueous solution. Synthesizing fluorescent carbon materials through spray drying method using aqueous BSA as a precursor is therefore economical.

The HMCSs were also suitable for loading drugs. In this study, ten different drugs including doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX), 5-fluorouracil (5-FU), paclitaxel (PTX), methylene blue (MB), sodium copper chlorophyllin (SCC), chlorin e6 (Ce6), cis-dichlorodiammineplatinum (II) (DDP), cyclophosphamide (CTX), quercitrin (QUE), and curcumin (CUR), were incorporated into HMCSs. DDP-loaded HMCSs were then selected for local treatment of nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC). Local radiotherapy is a widely used approach for the treatment of NPC [22–24]. However, since the nasal cavity is open to the outside, allowing easy access, photothermal therapy (PTT) is also a primary mode of treatment in NPC therapy [25–27].

Intratumoral injection with therapeutic reagents is a common administration route for local treatment [28–32]. To reduce the migration of reagents from the injection site to vital organs and tissues, such as the liver, the HMCSs developed in this work were designed on the micrometer scale. Microspheres with sizes in the range of several to tens of micrometers have been applied in both clinical [33,34] and fundamental research for the treatment of diseases through local injection (e.g., intratumoral injection) [35–39]. This administration route is safer for patients than intravenous injection.

The advantages of low-power density laser-triggered photothermal agent-mediated PTT, include laser irradiation being not only simple and convenient, but also highly efficient and safe, as only the targeted tissue containing the photothermal agent—such as tumor—generates a significant amount of heat after irradiation. Low-power density laser-triggered photothermal agent-mediated PTT is a highly specific therapeutic mode with minimal invasiveness. We show that the HMCSs were able to efficiently convert 980-nm laser light at low power density into heat energy, and this photothermal effect could be enhanced by microwave irradiation, leading to improved cancer PTT. Both 980-nm laser and microwaves—particularly microwaves—can penetrate into deep tissue. In addition, the temperature of target tissue under microwave irradiation usually increases in a short time. The microwave irradiation applied in this study is different to traditional microwave thermal therapy (MTT) [40,41] as well as the recently reported nanomaterial-mediated MTT [42–45].

The primary mechanism of traditional cancer MTT involves heating the tumor tissue through the vibration of water molecules in the tumor. Therefore, high power microwaves are used to achieve enhanced thermal therapy, which can increase the risk of damaging normal tissue [45]. For nanomaterial-mediated MTT, specific materials that can efficiently convert microwave energy into heat are chosen or designed [45–48], and the microwave probe must be placed very close (e.g., 1 cm [45]) to the fixed tumor-bearing mice.

In this study, however, microwaves were used to slightly enhance the local body temperature of the mice (1–2 °C), therefore high power microwaves were not required. The photothermal effect of HMCSs in mouse tumor under 980-nm laser irradiation was enhanced as a result of the higher initial tumor temperature induced by the microwave irradiation. As both the 980-nm laser and microwaves were low power, and only tumor tissue containing HMCSs can efficiently convert 980-nm laser light into heat, only tumor tissue was efficiently damaged by the enhanced thermal effect. The risk of the 980-nm laser and microwaves used in this study damaging normal tissue is low; no specific microwave-sensitive materials are required for this approach; and the distance between the microwave probe and tumor can be set to several centimeters or more, which is safe and convenient for future clinical use. Therefore, HMCSs and drug-loaded HMCSs, as well as the therapeutic strategy developed in this work, may have significant potential for use in local cancer therapy in the clinic.

In a typical experiment, aqueous BSA solution (100 mg/mL) was spray dried using a spray drier equipped with a rotary atomizer (Shanghai Da Chuanyuan Spray Drying Equipment Co., China). The inlet temperature and flow rate of the feed were 70 °C and 5 mL/min, respectively. After spray drying, the inlet temperature was kept at 70 °C under a flow of air for ~15 min to remove the excess water in the powder.

The dry powders were collected and heated at 150 °C for 2 h to remove the residual water and denature the BSA. The denatured BSA powder was placed in a small ceramic cup that was then inserted into the quartz glass tube of a furnace (SLG1100-60, Shanghai Shengli Test Instruments Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China). The furnace temperature was increased from room temperature to 250, 270, or 300 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min and maintained at these temperatures for 10–40 min under a flow of nitrogen. Subsequently, the powders (HMCSs) were allowed to cool naturally to room temperature.

To investigate the internal structure of the HMCSs, the HMCS powder was immersed in 100% acetone for 30 min, followed by sequential treatment with the following solutions; 70% acetone +30% EPON for 2 h, 30% acetone +70% EPON for ~12 h, and 100% EPON for ~12 h. The sample was then heated at 60 °C for 48 h. The embedded specimen was cooled naturally to room temperature and then cut into 70-nm ultrathin sections using an ultramicrotome (Leica Reichert Ultracut S Microtome StereoZoom 6, Wetzlar, Germany) for morphology evaluation. To investigate whether the HMCSs contained carbon dots, HMCSs dispersed in distilled water were ultrasonicated for 5, 30, and 60 min. The supernatants were then collected, and the morphology was evaluated and the fluorescence spectra were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the cell killing ability of HMCSs under 980-nm laser and/or microwave irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma cells (CNE cells) were seeded in three wells (B2, G2, and B11) (~1 × 104 cells/well) in a 96-well plate. After 24 h of incubation the medium was removed and HMCSs dispersed in PBS containing 15 wt% Pluronic F127 (abbreviated to F127) (100 μL, HMCS concentration: 1 mg/mL) were added to well B11 and G2. 100 μL of PBS was added to well B2. Only the cells in well B11 were then irradiated with a 980-nm laser (Shanghai Inter-Diff Optoelectronics Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) with low power density (0.28 W/cm2) for 20 min. After irradiation, the cells were quantitatively measured using CellTiter-Glo reagent. For qualitative detection, the cells were imaged using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon Eclipse TE2000, Japan). For quantitative measurement, the luminescence intensities of the cells were measured using a SpectraMax M5 universal microplate spectrophotometer (Molecular Devices Inc., CA, Sunnyvale, USA). Each experiment was repeated five times. (Note: HMCSs were not well dispersed in distilled water. Therefore, HMCSs in distilled water for the measurement of photothermal conversion and in PBS for the measurement of cell killing under irradiation, were dispersed with 15% F127. HMCSs could be well dispersed in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and in PBS containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) for more than 20 min when these suspensions contained 15% F127 (Fig. S1).

In addition, cells in a 96-well plate incubated with PBS-dispersed HMCSs or PBS were exposed to microwaves (SPW-1 Microwave Therapy Instrument, Shengpu Medical Equipment Technology, Xuzhou, China) (2450 MHz, 9 W) until the temperature of the cells increased to ~32 °C, and were then maintained at this temperature for 20 min. For other groups, the cells incubated with the above materials were first exposed to the microwaves until the medium temperature was maintained at ~32 °C, and then co-irradiated with the same microwaves and a 980-nm laser for 20 min. The distance between the microwave probe and the cells was maintained at 1 cm. The temperature of the cells before microwave and laser irradiation was maintained at ~25 °C. The cell viabilities were measured using the method described above.

The animal experiments were performed in accordance with the University of Tongjii institutional animal care and use committee guidelines. When the mouse CNE tumors of the nude mice reached 4 mm in diameter, 44 mice were selected for the experiments. The mice were divided into 11 groups (n = 4 for each group). Three groups were intratumorally injected with 100 μL of DDP-HMCS or HMCS suspension (all samples were dispersed in PBS containing 15 wt% F127) or DDP. The tumors were then exposed to a 3 W microwave source (frequency: 2450 MHz) until the tumor temperature increased to ~38 °C. This temperature was then maintained for 20 min by the microwaves. This treatment was repeated daily for each mouse (total: 18 days). The distance between the microwave probe and the tumors was maintained at 3.5 cm.

Two groups were intratumorally injected with 100 μL of DDP-HMCS or HMCS suspension, and then irradiated with a 980-nm laser (power density: 0.28 W/cm2) (20 min for each mouse per day). Once the tumors were no longer detected the laser irradiation was stopped.

The next two groups were also intratumorally injected with 100 μL of DDP-HMCS or HMCS suspension. These tumors were first exposed to 3 W microwaves until the tumor temperature increased to 38 °C and was maintained this temperature. They were then irradiated with the 980-nm laser for 20 min. During laser irradiation, the tumors were also continuously exposed to the 3 W microwave source. Each mouse underwent this treatment daily until the tumors were no longer detected.
The remaining four groups were intratumorally injected with 100 μL of DDP-HMCS, HMCS, DDP, or F127. These tumors were not exposed to laser or microwave irradiation.

The mice were euthanized at 19 days post-injection and the tumors or skin at the original tumor site (in cases where the tumor was no longer present after treatment) were resected for analysis, including measurements of tumor weight and size, as well as histological observation. For histological analysis, the tissues were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, embedded in paraffin wax, cut into ultrathin 7-μm sections using an ultramicrotome (Leica RM2126RT, Germany), stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and then observed using an upright microscope (LeicaDM2500, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and retention of HMCSs in mouse after local injection
ce:section.ce:para: PBS (containing 15% F127)-dispersed HMCSs were subcutaneously injected into four nude mice (5 mg/mL, 100 μL for each mouse). The injected sites were irradiated with a 980-nm laser 1, 7, 15, and 30 days post-injection (10 min each time). The temperatures were measured using a thermal imager. At 30 days post-injection, the four mice were euthanized and the tissues at the injection sites were resected and imaged using a digital camera. The resected tissues were fixed, embedded, cut into ultrathin sections, and stained with H&E for evaluation. Ultrathin sections of tissue from the injection sites and main organs including heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney without H&E staining were also evaluated using a fluorescence microscope (LeicaDM2500, Germany). In addition, HMCSs were carefully separated from the resected tissues using surgical scissors and tweezers. The separated HMCSs were observed using SEM and a laser scanning confocal microscope (TCSNT: Leica, Nussloch, Germany) (excitation filter: 365 nm, emission filter: 400–600 nm). The detailed procedures of other experiments including some cell and animal experiments were described in the Supporting Information.

Significant differences between groups were determined using the one-way ANOVA test in Excel 2013 (Microsoft, Redmond, USA). A P-value <0.05 was considered statistically significant, P < 0.01 was considered highly significant, P < 0.001 was considered very highly significant. Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation.

The carbonization of the BSA particles was primarily confirmed by observation with the naked eye (Fig. 1 a). The as-prepared BSA particles and HMCSs prepared by carbonization of the BSA particles were all spherical in shape (Fig. 1b–e and Fig. S2). The presence of several broken BSA spheres and HMCSs in the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images implies that these spheres may be hollow in structure (see inserts in the images). Most of the HMCS particles prepared at 300 °C for 30 and 40 min were badly aggregated (Fig. 1e and Fig. S2). The majority of the HMCSs carbonized at lower temperature (e.g., 270 °C) or at 300 °C for 20 min existed as single particles (Fig. 1c and d).

To observe the internal structure of the HMCSs, the samples prepared at 300 °C for 20 min were sliced using an ultrathin sectioning technique. The results showed that the HMCSs contained a large internal cavity (Fig. 1f), and that many small holes, primarily in the 1–10 nm diameter range, were distributed in the shell (Fig. 1g and h), these findings were confirmed by the transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images, respectively. The large internal cavities and numerous mesopores in the shells are beneficial for drug loading. Another notable finding was that numerous dark dots of no more than 10 nm in diameter were trapped inside the shells (Fig. 1h and i). As the BSA used in this study was high purity, these dots may be carbon dots. Upon careful observation, the dots show little or negligible evidence of lattice fringes (Fig. 1i), which indicates that the carbon dots may be amorphous in structure.

The size distribution of the HMCSs was slightly shifted to smaller sizes compared with that of the BSA spheres before carbonization. For example, the diameter of the HMCSs prepared at 300 °C for 20 min ranged mainly from 5 to 25 μm, while that of the BSA microspheres before carbonization ranged from 5 to 30 μm.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, BSA spheres have a sharp absorption peak at ~206 nm, and a broad but low absorption from ~230 to 1100 nm. After carbonization, the peak intensities at both shorter and longer wavelengths significantly increased. In addition, the intensity of the absorption peaks over ~350 nm in wavelength increased with increasing carbonization temperature (e.g., from 250 °C to 300 °C for 20 min) and with prolonged carbonization time (e.g., at 300 °C from 10 to 30 min). This indicates that the molecular structure of BSA was significantly altered and more monatomic carbons may be formed after carbonization at high temperature. It should be noted that the intensity of the absorption peak over ~350 nm for HMCSs prepared at 300 °C for 20 min was only slightly lower than that of those treated for 30 min, which implies that the degree of carbonization of BSA spheres treated at 300 °C for 20 min may be close to the maximum attainable. As a 980-nm laser was selected to trigger the HMCSs to generate heat for killing cells, using the Lambert-Beer Law we calculated the mass extinction coefficient of HMCSs prepared at 300 °C for 20 min to be 2.57 L·g−1 cm−1 at a wavelength of 980 nm.

The FT-IR spectra of the BSA spheres before and after carbonization further indicate that “300 °C, 20 min” may be suitable conditions for the preparation of HMCSs with a high degree of carbonization. As shown in Fig. 2b, the BSA spheres exhibit complex peaks in the Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum, particularly at lower wavenumbers from ~1800 to 400 cm−1. Many peaks in the FT-IR spectrum of the BSA spheres were no longer detected after carbonization, or their intensities significantly decreased. For example, the peaks assigned to amide I (1700–1600 cm−1), amide II (1600–1500 cm−1), and amide III (1220–1330 cm−1) [50–52] associated with α-helix and β-sheet structures were significantly changed or not detected after carbonization at 300 °C for 30 min, and a similar observation was made for samples carbonized at 300 °C for 20 min.

As HMCSs prepared at 300 °C for 20 min exhibited a high degree of carbonization and less aggregation than those prepared over 30 or 40 min time periods—confirmed by the morphology, and absorption and FT-IR spectra described above—the HMCSs prepared under these conditions (300 °C, 20 min) were selected for the subsequent cancer therapy studies.

For HMCSs prepared at 300 °C for 20 min, the weight and number percentages of carbon (C) atoms were 92.26% and 93.79% of the total atoms, respectively (Fig. 2c). Only a small amount of oxygen (O) (number: 3.71%), nitrogen (N) (number: 2.48%), and phosphorus (P) (number: 0.01%) atoms were detected in the sample. This indicates that the HMCSs were well carbonized and may therefore have good biocompatibility as a result of their high purity.

The Raman spectrum of the HMCSs showed that the carbon spheres are mainly in the amorphous phase (Fig. 2d). However, two weak peaks at ~1350 cm−1 (D-band) and ~1580 cm−1 (G-band) also appeared in the Raman spectrum of the HMCSs. These two peaks are attributed to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms (the latter are in aromatic ring terminations), respectively. This indicates that weak graphitic structures may be present in the HMCSs. Two broad peaks with intensity maxima at 2θ = 21.9° and 41.9° and two weak peaks at 9.0° and 13.0° appear in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 2e). Only the peak at 13.0° may be assigned to the hexagonal structure of graphite [53], which further indicates that the HMCSs are mainly in the amorphous phase.

N2 adsorption–desorption results show that the size of the pores in the HMCSs ranges from 0.8 to 10 nm (most are no more than 5 nm) (Fig. 2f). The surface area and total pore volume were 37.67 m2/g and 0.042 cm3/g, respectively. The hollow mesoporous carbon spheres are therefore suitable for loading small molecule drugs.

Both the BSA spheres and HMCSs showed blue or green fluorescence in distilled water (Fig. 2g). As shown in Fig. 2h, compared with the fluorescence emission of the BSA spheres, that of the resulting HMCSs had a significantly higher intensity and the maximum emission shifted to red by 34 nm after the BSA spheres were carbonized at 250 °C for 20 min. After the BSA spheres were carbonized at 270 °C for 20 min, the emission intensity further increased, but the wavelength did not show a clear shift. Compared with the fluorescence emission of HMCSs prepared at 270 °C for 20 min, the emission intensity of the HMCSs prepared at 300 °C significantly decreased with increasing carbonization time. As shown in Fig. 2g and h, to obtain HMCSs with bright fluorescence, the carbonization time should be no more than 30 min when the carbonization temperature is maintained at 300 °C.

The fluorescence of HMCSs is useful for conveniently identifying their spatial structure. As shown in Fig. 2i and j, confocal fluorescence imaging not only shows that each HMCS carbonized at 300 °C for 20 min emits bright fluorescence, but also demonstrates, through the sequential optical section images, that all of the HMCSs are hollow in structure, as the fluorescence at the edge of the HMCSs is much brighter than that in the center. The fluorescence of HMCSs may also be useful for detecting heavy metal ions in aqueous solution through the quenching of fluorescence by specific heavy metal ions (Fig. S3), however the use of HMCSs as sensing probes for detecting heavy metal ions is not a focus of this study.

The bright fluorescence emitted by each HMCS indicates that the spheres may be composed of fluorescent carbon dots. As detailed above (see Fig. 1h and i), the dark dots within the shells of the HMCSs may be the carbon dots. The conversion of BSA molecules into fluorescent carbon dots by other techniques, such as via hydrolysis in concentrated sulfuric acid at 50 °C for at least 2 h [54], and hydrothermal treatment at 195 °C for 6 h [55], or 200 °C for 10 h [56], has also been reported. Compared with the previous reports [54–56], the preparation of HMCSs containing fluorescent carbon dots reported here does not require strong acid, and is simple and highly efficient.

To further demonstrate that HMCSs contain fluorescent carbon dots, water-dispersed HMCSs prepared at 300 °C for 20 min were sonicated for 5, 30, or 60 min. The suspensions were then centrifuged and the supernatant fractions were collected for evaluation. The results showed that the color of the corresponding supernatant solutions changed from light to dark (Fig. 3 a) and the fluorescence intensity of the solutions increased with increasing sonication time (Fig. 3b). The fluorescence peak shifted to red as the excitation wavelength was increased, which indicates that the supernatant solution may have contained different-sized carbon dots (Fig. 3c). The HRTEM image shows that the supernatant solution contained a large amount of nanodots with diameters in the range 1–10 nm (Fig. 3d). These nanodots may be carbon dots released and cut from the HMCSs. Careful observation showed that only weak lattice fringes could be seen in these carbon dots (Fig. 3e). Compared with carbon dots prepared from other carbon sources containing graphite structure [57] or aromatic structure [58], carbon dots prepared using BSA as the precursor exhibited a low degree of graphitization. Upon 671-nm or 980-nm laser irradiation, carbon dots incubated with CNE cells generated intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Fig. 3f). This effect could be used to kill cancer cells under red or near-infrared laser irradiation.

A possible mechanism for the formation of carbon dots in the HMCSs may be as follows: Heat and mass transfers occur simultaneously during the spray drying of the droplets generated by the spray dryer [59,60]. BSA then precipitates from solution at the droplet surface as the heat transfers from the surface to the center of the droplet. The precipitated BSA then gradually forms a shell and high-concentration BSA in the shell diffuses to the microsphere center during spray drying owing to the BSA concentration gradient. The residual solution in the center of the particles with shells continuously evaporates as a result of the high temperature and pressure internal environment during spray drying, which results in a large number of mesopores and smaller or larger pores being formed in the microspheres. These pores may then influence the diffusion of BSA from the shell to the microsphere center during spray drying, which would cause BSA to precipitate inhomogeneously in the un-dried microspheres resulting in much denser precipitates forming in some micro-regions. When the BSA microspheres are denatured at 150 °C and carbonized at 300 °C, these dense precipitates in the micro-regions become more compact owing to further dehydration, and then shrink into carbon dots or break down into carbon dots after carbonization at high temperature. This carbon dot formation mechanism is similar to that resulting from pyrolysis of biomass waste at high temperature [61,62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the cytotoxicity of HMCSs and four typical carbon-based materials; C60, MWCNTs, rGO, and CNHs
ce:section.ce:para: C60, MWCNTs, rGO, and CNHs are typical quasi-zero-dimensional, one dimensional, two dimensional, and three dimensional carbon materials, respectively, which have been widely investigated for biomedical applications. After CNE cells were incubated with 0.1 mg/mL MWCNTs, rGO, or CNHs for 24 h, the cell viability significantly decreased from ~100% to (86.12 ± 1.97)%, (89.66 ± 2.54)%, and (85.52 ± 2.18)%, respectively, (P < 0.01, compared with untreated cells), and at higher concentration (0.4 mg/mL), the cell viability showed further significant decrease to (82.56 ± 2.31)%, (82.21 ± 4.82)%, and (81.91 ± 2.30)%, respectively, (P < 0.001, compared with untreated cells) (Fig. 4 a). However, when the cells were incubated with HMCSs or C60 under the same conditions, the viability remained over 96% (for 0.1 mg/mL) or decreased to (95.10 ± 3.98)% or (94.50 ± 3.17)%, respectively, (for 0.4 mg/mL) (P < 0.05, compared with cells without treatment). C60 exhibited low cytotoxicity, which may be a result of the high degree of aggregation of the C60 nanoparticles caused by their ultra-small size (0.71 nm in diameter [63]).

C60, MWCNTs, rGO, and CNHs were all found to cause cell-cycle defects in CNE cancer cells; however, few side effects on the cell-cycle distribution were detected for HMCSs (Fig. 4b and c). When CNE cells were incubated with the four carbon materials described above and HMCSs (0.4 mg/mL for all samples) for 24 h, a higher population of the cells incubated with the four carbon materials were arrested in the G0/G1 phase compared with the no treatment group (among the four carbon materials, C60 exhibited the smallest effect on the cell-cycle distribution); in contrast, the cell-cycle distribution in the HMCS group was similar to that in the untreated group. These findings indicate that HMCSs may have the lowest cytotoxicity among the five carbon materials tested.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HMCS drug loading capacity and drug release
ce:section.ce:para: Ten drugs commonly used for cancer therapy—including DOX, 5-FU, PTX, CTX, MB, SCC, Ce6, DDP, CUR, and QUE—were all successfully incorporated into the HMCSs. The loading efficiency of the drugs in the HMCSs was calculated (Fig. 4d) based on the standard curves of the ten drugs (Fig. S4). Among the water-soluble drugs, MB not only has the lowest molecular weight (319.86) and smallest size (length: 1.43 nm, width: 0.61 nm, and height: 0.4 nm), but also has a positive charge. The zeta potential of the HMCSs was found to be −32.17 mV, therefore, the highest loading capacity of HMCSs was found for MB—100 mg of HMCSs could load (42.8 ± 2.7) mg of MB. PTX is a water-insoluble drug and its molecular weight (853.91) is the highest of the drugs tested, which may account for the loading of PTX into HMCSs being the lowest—100 mg of HMCSs could only load (19.3 ± 4.2) mg of PTX.

As SCC has very bright red fluorescence with a maximum emission at 662 nm (Fig. S5), this drug was selected to investigate the location of drugs loaded into the HMCSs. We found that the SCC-loaded HMCS aqueous suspension and all SCC-HMCSs emitted bright red fluorescence (Fig. 4e and Fig. S5). Sequential optical section images further showed that SCC molecules were located in both the shell and internal void of the HMCSs (but mainly in the shell) (Fig. 4f). Under the same imaging conditions, HMCSs alone were found to exhibit no red fluorescence (Fig. 4g and Fig. S5). As only (74.6 ± 3.88)% of the SCC loaded was released after the SCC-HMCSs were dialyzed against water for 120 h (Fig. S6a), SCC was found to exhibit a sustained release profile. Other drugs such as DDP also exhibited sustained release from the drug-loaded HMCSs [(69.1 ± 2.20)% DDP was released after 120 h of dialysis] (Fig. S6b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature increase of HMCSs upon 980-nm laser and microwave irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 a, the temperature of HMCS aqueous suspensions (containing 15 wt% F127) under 980-nm laser irradiation could be increased by extending the irradiation time (from 0 to 20 min), or increasing the concentration of HMCSs (from 0.5 to 1 mg/mL). In contrast, the temperature of the distilled water and water-dissolved F127 controls increased much more slowly upon 980-nm laser irradiation. F127 is therefore not a 980-nm laser light-absorbing material, as no clear difference between the temperature increases of the water and water-dissolved F127 groups could be detected.

HMCSs in mouse tumor also rapidly converted 980-nm laser light into heat (Fig. 5b). The temperature of the tumor (size: ~4 mm) injected with 50 μL of HMCSs (HMCS concentration: 5 mg/mL, F127: 15 wt%) increased from normal body temperature to 45.53 ± 0.25 °C upon irradiation for 20 min with a 980-nm laser, which was ~6.26 °C higher than that of tumor injected with PBS under the same irradiation conditions.

Although the temperatures of the HMCS aqueous suspension and mouse tumor injected with HMCSs both increased rapidly under microwave irradiation, the microwave thermal conversion rates were similar to those of the controls (distilled water, dispersion agent F127, or tumor injected with PBS exposed to microwaves) (Fig. 5c and d). This indicates that both HMCSs and F127 are poor microwave-absorbing materials. However, when the same HMCS aqueous suspension was exposed to microwaves until they increased to and maintained a certain temperature (e.g., 28 °C or 30 °C), the thermal conversion of the suspension under co-irradiation with laser and microwave sources increased more rapidly than that under laser irradiation alone (Fig. 5e). The greater the initial temperature difference between the groups exposed to the laser alone and those exposed to the combination of laser and microwaves, the greater the final temperature difference. For example, when the difference in the initial temperature was ~6.0 °C, the difference in the temperatures after 20 min of laser irradiation and co-irradiation was ~8.6 °C. Similar results were obtained for mouse tumor injected with HMCSs under the same laser and microwave co-irradiation (Fig. 5f).

This observation indicates that the photothermal effect of HMCSs in aqueous solution and tumor were improved with the assistance of microwave irradiation. This is important for cancer PTT, as less PTT agent and/or a lower power density laser can be used to achieve high efficiency PTT compared with those used in low efficiency PTT. PTT using low-dose photothermal agents and/or low power density lasers is safe for animal tissues [64–70].

The primary reason for the photothermal effect of HMCSs being improved by microwave irradiation may be that the photothermal effect is predominantly a result of the oscillations of the electrons in materials under laser irradiation. When the HMCSs in aqueous solution or in tumor are exposed to microwaves, water around the HMCSs absorbs the microwave energy and rapidly generates heat. Ions in animal tissue, such as Na+, also absorb the microwaves and produce heat. Although HMCSs are not microwave-sensitive materials, the temperature of the HMCSs increases as a result of heat conduction from the microwave-sensitive water and ions, and the molecular and electronic thermal motions in the heated HMCSs are enhanced. Therefore, oscillations of the electrons in HMCSs caused by laser irradiation may become easier to induce or more intense, and more electrons may participate in vibration. More heat produced by the vibrated electrons is therefore generated under co-irradiation with both laser and microwave sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell killing efficiency of HMCSs and DDP-HMCSs upon 980-nm laser, microwave, and Co-irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: The photothermal effect of HMCSs induced by 980-nm laser irradiation showed a high capacity for cell killing (Fig. 6 a). The temperature of cells before irradiation was 25 °C. Only (76.69 ± 7.41)% of CNE cells survived after incubation with 1 mg/mL of HMCSs for ~5 min followed by exposure to the 980-nm laser for 20 min. Upon microwave irradiation at 9 W, HMCSs had little effect on the cancer cell viability [the viability was (97.40 ± 4.46)%]. However, when the HMCS-incubated cells were first exposed to microwaves until the temperature of the culture was maintained at 32 °C, and then co-irradiated with the 980-nm laser and microwaves for 20 min, the cell viability significantly decreased to (57.32 ± 2.52)%. This indicates that although microwaves alone were unable to trigger the HMCSs to kill cancer cells, microwaves could significantly improve the cell killing efficiency of the photothermal effect of HMCSs.

As the cell killing efficiency of DDP is time dependent, CNE cells were incubated with DDP-HMCSs for 24 h (not ~5 min as described above), and then exposed to the 980-nm laser and/or microwaves. As shown in Fig. 6b, the cells in the “DDP-HMCSs, laser” group were killed by the synergistic effect between the photothermal effect of HMCSs and the cytotoxicity of DDP [cell viability: (50.65 ± 1.92)%]. This synergistic effect was further improved when the cells incubated with DDP-HMCSs were co-irradiated with the laser and microwaves [cell viability: (25.16 ± 2.32) %]. (74.20 ± 3.73)% of cells survived after the cells were incubated with DDP (0.2 mg/mL) alone for 24 h. Neither laser irradiation alone nor co-irradiation with laser and microwaves improved the cytotoxicity of DDP.

Prior to the in vivo cancer therapy experiments we investigated the serum stability of the HMCSs. We immersed HMCS powders in 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) for one month and found that the HMCS morphology showed no obvious changes during the immersion (Fig. 7 a). Careful observation showed that no spheres were damaged, and the sphere sizes remained the same even after mixing with FBS for 30 days. In addition, the 980-nm laser triggered-photothermal conversion of HMCSs after immersion in FBS for one month was unchanged compared with that of HMCSs treated with FBS for less time or without FBS treatment (Fig. 7b). These results indicate that the HMCSs are very stable in serum, which is important for long-term in vivo cancer phototherapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo cancer therapy using HMCSs and DDP-HMCSs with 980-nm laser and/or microwave irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 8 a and b, intratumoral injection with 50 μL of aqueous HMCS suspension containing F127 (HMCS concentration: 5 mg/mL, F127 concentration: 15 wt%) slightly inhibited tumor growth compared with intratumoral injection with F127 alone (control group). However, no statistical difference in tumor growth was observed between the two groups. In contrast, when the mouse tumors were intratumorally injected with the same HMCS suspensions and then irradiated with the 980-nm laser (“HMCSs, laser” group), all mouse tumors (n = 4) showed clear damage (Fig. 8a), and the tumor growth was significantly inhibited compared with the control group after only 3 days of irradiation (P < 0.05) (Fig. 8b and c). After 9 days of irradiation, two tumors were no longer detected, and after 10 days of irradiation the remaining two tumors were also not detected. This indicates that the photothermal effect of HMCSs triggered by the 980-nm laser showed a strong ability to inhibit tumor growth.

The irradiation was stopped as soon as the tumors were no longer detected. However, in the “HMCSs, laser” group, during the following 8 or 9 days of no irradiation two tumors re-grew at the original tumor sites at 15 days after the injection of HMCSs. This implies that although no tumors could be observed or detected through touch after 9 or 10 days of 980-nm laser irradiation, as described above, if a small number of cancer cells were not killed, these cells were able to act as seeds for the subsequent development of tumors once the photothermal stimuli were stopped. To address this issue, we combined the laser and microwaves to enhance the cell killing efficiency by improving the thermal effect in the tumor.

When the mouse tumors were injected with the HMCSs as described above (HMCS concentration: 5 mg/mL, F127 concentration: 15 wt%), and then exposed to microwaves until the tumor temperature reached and was finally maintained at 38 °C (“HMCSs, microwave” group), no clear inhibition of tumor growth was observed compared with the control (Fig. 8a and b). This is a result of the thermal effect caused by the microwave irradiation not being sufficient to damage the tumor tissues.

However, when the mouse tumors injected with HMCSs were exposed to microwaves using the method described above, and then co-irradiated with the 980-nm laser and microwaves for 20 min (“HMCSs, laser + microwave” group), no mouse tumors (n = 4) could be detected after only 5 days of co-irradiation, which was 4–5 days faster than the “HMCSs, laser” group (Fig. 8a–c). In addition, no tumors recovered in the following 13 days when no laser or microwave irradiation was applied.

This is an interesting and important result as HMCSs exposed to microwave irradiation alone were unable to inhibit tumor growth, however microwaves provided assistance to the cancer PTT effect of HMCSs to significantly accelerate the tumor inhibition, which could be beneficial in clinical cancer therapy.

The tumor inhibition efficiency was also significantly improved by loading the HMCSs with anticancer drugs and by laser irradiation or “laser + microwave” co-irradiation. As shown in Fig. 8a and b, tumor growth was significantly inhibited by the chemotherapy of DDP; however the introduction of microwave irradiation did not improve the tumor inhibition rate (“DDP, microwave” group), and a similar result was observed for the “DDP-HMCSs” and “DDP-HMCSs, microwave” groups. However, as shown in Fig. 8a, b, and d, the mouse tumors treated with DDP-HMCSs and the 980-nm laser (“DDP-HMCSs, laser” group) were not detected after 7 days of irradiation, and for the “DDP-HMCSs, laser + microwave” group, no tumors were detected after only 3 days of irradiation. In addition, none of the tumors in these two groups recovered when the laser and microwave irradiation were stopped. These observations indicate that the cancer cells in these tumor tissues were completely killed by the synergistic effect of chemotherapy and thermal treatment, and this synergistic effect resulting from co-irradiation with laser and microwaves is more significant than the effect of laser irradiation alone.

At 19 days post injection, the tumors were resected for further analysis. As shown in Fig. 8e, the tumor sizes in the “DDP-HMCSs, MW”, “DDP, MW”, and DDP-HMCSs and DDP without irradiation groups were not statistically different from each other, but were significantly smaller than those in the control group. A similar result was found for the “HMCSs, MW”, and HMCSs and F127 without irradiation groups. In the “HMCSs, laser” group, only two small tumors were observed, and only skin could be resected from the original tumor site in the following three groups: “HMCSs, laser + MW”, “DDP-HMCSs, laser”, and “DDP-HMCSs, laser + MW”. These results are further supported by the average tumor weight analysis (Fig. 8f). In the eight groups for which tumors could be detected as described above, a large amount of vigorous-growth cancer cells were observed at the tumor tissues (Fig. S7); and in the three groups for which tumors were no longer detected as described above, no cancer cells could be observed in the tissues resected from the original tumor site, which was confirmed by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining images of ultra-thin tissue sections (Fig. 8g). It should be noted that both of near-infrared laser- and microwave-triggered photothermal agent-mediated cancer treatments show less affinity for killing remote metastatic cancer cells.

One key issue determining whether the developed materials have potential for clinical translation is their in vivo toxicity. To evaluate the in vivo toxicity of HMCSs we locally injected HMCSs into ICR mice using the same method as described for in vivo cancer therapy. The results showed that the mouse body weight and main organ coefficient at different times after injection (from 1 to 30 days) were all similar to those of mice not injected with HMCSs (Fig. 9 a and b). Furthermore, the cellular structures of the main organs—including heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney—of the mice exposed to HMCSs all exhibited normal morphology, which was confirmed by the optical images of H&E stained tissue sections (Fig. 9c). These findings imply that mouse health is not greatly influenced by exposure to the HMCSs.

To further investigate the health of the mice, mouse blood was extracted for analysis. It is notable that the levels of nine important serum biochemistry parameters—including albumin (ALB), globulin (GLOB), albumin/globulin ratio (A/G), total protein (TP), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), urea, and creatinine (CRE)—of HMCSs-exposed mice at 1, 7, and 30 days after injection, all showed no statistical differences from those of mice without HMCSs exposure (Fig. 10 a). The levels of seven representative hematology parameters—including white blood cells (WBC), red blood cells (RBC), hemoglobin (HGB), mean corpuscular volume (MCV), mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH), platelets (PLT), and hematocrit (HCT)—of the HMCS-exposed mice at different times after injection were also all similar to those of mice without HMCS exposure (Fig. 10b).

These results indicate that the mouse liver and kidney functions, as well as other physiological indexes and the histological structures of main organs, remained normal even after the mice were exposed to HMCSs for 30 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term retention and structural stability of HMCSs in tissues at the injection sites and clearance from the body
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate why HMCSs exhibit little toxicity in mice after local injection, and how HMCSs are cleared from the body, we locally injected HMCSs into nude mice using the same method as that described for the in vivo cancer therapy. We found that black sediment could be clearly observed at the injection sites after administration, and the black sediment remained entrapped inside the injection tissue throughout the 30 days of monitoring (Fig. 11 a). When the tissues containing black sediment were irradiated with a 980-nm laser, the temperature of the irradiated tissues increased rapidly. Interestingly, there was no clear difference in the photothermal conversion profiles of the in vivo black sediment at 1, 7, 15, or 30 days post-injection (Fig. 11b). These observations imply that after injection HMCSs did not leave the injection sites, which is similar to stent implantation for nasopharyngeal tumor therapy [71,72]. Only distributing at the injection site after intratumoral injection is a useful feature for long-term local cancer therapy and is important for reducing the in vivo toxicity.

At 30 days post-injection the tissues at the injection sites were resected and the black sediment was exposed to air (Fig. 12 a). It could be seen that the black sediment was confined to a small region. These regions represent the injection sites. H&E stained images of ultra-thin sections showed that a large amount of HMCSs were entrapped by the tissue at the injection site (Fig. 12b). Careful observation showed no free HMCSs in the tissue near the black sediment (Fig. 12b and c). HMCSs in these ultra-thin sections emitted bright fluorescence, and this fluorescence was noticeably different to the weak blue fluorescence emitted by the tissue (Fig. S7). The ultra-thin sections of liver, spleen, lung, and kidney of the HMCS-injected mice described above only emitted weak blue fluorescence; no HMCS fluorescence could be detected in these main organ sections (Fig. S8). These results further indicated that HMCSs were only distributed at the injection sites after local injection. In addition, the HMCSs entrapped by the tissue maintained their original spherical shape and hollow structure (Fig. 12c and d and Fig. S7).

We found that HMCSs could be separated from the tissue through a simple surgical procedure. The confocal fluorescence images showed that nearly all of the separated microspheres still emitted blue fluorescence and also showed hollow profiles (Fig. 12e and f), which were similar to those of HMCSs before injection. SEM images demonstrated that the surfaces of HMCSs 30 days post-injection maintained their original morphology despite being coated with mouse tissue for a prolonged period (Fig. 12g and insert). These results further confirmed that HMCSs could be retained long-term by the tissues at the injection sites after local injection, and exhibited very stable structures. This is because the HMCSs developed in this study are microscale in size and mainly composed of carbon atoms; such microspheres have no opportunities to leave the injection site and penetrate into deep tissue through the intercellular space, and are also difficult to degrade in vivo. This is the primary reason why the mice exposed to HMCSs retained their health. Therefore, in this study, the administration route of HMCSs for inhibiting tumor growth is through local injection, as it is safe and has been used in the clinic [33,34] and in fundamental research [35–39].

Fluorescent carbon spheres with hollow mesoporous structures (HMCSs) containing fluorescent carbon dots were prepared using BSA as a precursor and a spray drying combined with high temperature carbonization technique. This method is simple, safe, ‘green’, and highly efficient, and does not require organic solvents, strong acid or alkali, or strong oxidants, making it suitable for producing fluorescent porous carbon for biomedical applications in bulk. The HMCSs obtained showed low cytotoxicity compared with fullerene C60, MWCNTs, rGO, and CNHs, and were stable in serum. HMCSs persisted at the injection site long-term after local injection, and could be easily and completely removed from the tissue using a surgical procedure. In addition, mouse health was essentially unaffected by local injection of HMCSs. Based on the drug-loading capacities of ten different drugs, the HMCSs were found to be good carriers for drug loading. In addition, the HMCSs rapidly converted 980-nm laser light into heat energy, and cancer cells and mouse tumors were significantly damaged by the photothermal effect. We developed a strategy for significantly improving the thermal effect of HMCSs through the combination of 980-nm laser and microwave irradiation. HMCSs are not microwave-sensitive materials, therefore cancer cells and mouse tumors were not damaged by HMCSs under microwave irradiation. However, when the photothermal effect of HMCSs was assisted by microwave irradiation, the growth of mouse tumors was significantly inhibited and tumors shrank to the point where they were no longer detected. This effect was found to be much more efficient than photothermal treatment without the assistance of microwave irradiation. The synergistic therapeutic effect of the PTT of HMCSs and chemotherapy of DDP in DDP-loaded HMCSs, was also significantly improved by co-irradiation with a laser and microwaves. Owing to the low cytotoxicity of HMCSs, the facile application of 980-nm laser and microwave co-irradiation, and the highly efficient inhibition of tumor growth; we believe that the reported HMCSs and associated approach for inhibiting tumor growth may have potential in clinical cancer therapy.
The data used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon request.
